Given some categorized constants in the:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
wines("chianti"; "port"; "riesling"; "shiraz").
price(24; 25; 26; 27).
name("Isabel"; "Kurt"; "Priscilla"; "Robin").

% For each name, it matches with exactly 1 wine and 1 price.
{match(Name, Wine, Price): wines(Wine), price(Price)}=1 :- name(Name).
```

names("Celia"; "Mandy"; "Raymond"; "Tom").

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
wins(24; 27; 30; 33).
boxer("Edna Easton"; "Irma"; "Jane"; "Sonia").
losses(2; 4; 8; 12).

% Define the predicate boxer_stats(Boxer, Wins, Losses)
% Each boxer has exactly one (Wins, Losses) pair.
{boxer_stats(B, W, L) : wins(W), losses(L)} = 1 :- boxer(B).

% Constraint: boxer_stats(Edna Easton, W2, 2) and boxer_stats(Irma, W8, 8) with W2 = W8 + 3
:- boxer_stats("Edna Easton", W2, 2), boxer_stats("Irma", W8, 8), W2 != W8 + 3.

% Disjunctive fact: Sonia has either (33, L_S) or (27, L_S) for some L_S
boxer_stats("Sonia", 33, L_S) ; boxer_stats("Sonia", 27, L_S) :- losses(L_S).

% Ensure consistency: boxer_stats is total and unique per boxer
% (already enforced by the cardinality constraint above)
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
days(270; 274; 278; 282).
boat_type("catamaran"; "ketch"; "schooner"; "trimaran").
sailor("Debra Decker"; "Gil Baxter"; "Vicky Estes"; "Wendell Orr").

% Each sailor sailed on exactly one boat on exactly one day.
{sailor_sailed(S, B, D): boat_type(B), days(D)}=1 :- sailor(S).

% Fixed facts:
sailor_sailed("Vicky Estes", "catamaran", D1) :- true.

% Constraints:
% Debra Decker sailed on some boat B_d on day D_d
% Wendell Orr sailed on some boat B on day 278
% So, D_d = D_t - 4, where D_t is the day Wendell Orr sailed on trimaran
% But Wendell Orr sailed on day 278, so D_t = 278
% Therefore, D_d = 278 - 4 = 274
sailor_sailed("Debra Decker", B_d, 274) :- sailor_sailed("Gil Baxter", "trimaran", 278).

% Vicky Estes sailed on some boat B_v on day D_v
% Some sailor S_s sailed on schooner on day D_s
% D_s = D_v + 4
% Vicky Estes sailed on catamaran, so B_v = "catamaran"
% So D_s = D_v + 4
% We already have D_v from the first constraint: D_v = D1
% So D_s = D1 + 4
% But D_s must be one of 270, 274, 278, 282
% So D1 must be 270, 274, or 278

% We enforce the constraints:
:- sailor_sailed("Vicky Estes", "catamaran", D_v), 
   sailor_sailed(S_s, "schooner", D_s), 
   D_s != D_v + 4, 
   sailor(S_s).

% We enforce the fixed assignments:
sailor_sailed("Wendell Orr", B, 278) :- true.

% We enforce the chain: Debra Decker on day 274, and Wendell Orr on trimaran on day 278
% So Debra Decker must be on day 274, and the trimaran must be sailed on day 278
% So we can infer:
:- sailor_sailed("Debra Decker", B_d, D_d), D_d != 274.

% We allow multiple rules to be defined, but the above constraints are sufficient to generate the search space
% The system will generate all possible assignments satisfying the constraints.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
years(2007; 2008; 2009; 2010).
superhero("Criminal Bane"; "Deep Shadow"; "Ultra Hex"; "Wonderman").
name("Gabe Grant"; "Ivor Ingram"; "Matt Minkle"; "Peter Powers").

% Define the predicate patrols(Name, Superhero, Year) with constraints.
% Each name patrols exactly one superhero in each year, but we only define possible assignments.
% The following rule generates all possible combinations of patrols, with the given constraints.

% For each name and year, assign exactly one superhero they patrol.
{patrols(Name, Superhero, Year) : superhero(Superhero)} = 1 :- name(Name), years(Year).

% Constraint: "Gabe Grant" patrols "Ultra Hex" in some year.
patrols("Gabe Grant", "Ultra Hex", Y) :- years(Y).

% Constraint: "Criminal Bane" is patrolled by some name in 2007 or 2009.
patrols(Name, "Criminal Bane", 2007) ; patrols(Name, "Criminal Bane", 2009) :- name(Name).

% Constraint: If Peter Powers patrols in year Y1 and Matt Minkle patrols in year Y2, then Y1 < Y2.
:- patrols("Peter Powers", _, Y1), patrols("Matt Minkle", _, Y2), Y1 >= Y2.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
days(20; 21; 22; 23).
companies("Canway"; "Haytouch"; "Laneplex"; "Sancode").
positions("copywriter"; "janitor"; "sales rep"; "web developer").

% Each day D, company C, and position P can be part of an interview, but constraints limit the possibilities.
% The interview relation is defined over day, company, and position.
interview(D, C, P) :- days(D), companies(C), positions(P).

% Constraint: interview on day D at "Sancode" implies that the same day must have a "copywriter" interview.
interview(D, "Sancode", P) :- interview(D, "Sancode", P), positions(P).
:- interview(D, "Sancode", P), interview(D, C, "copywriter"), D = D, not interview(D, C, "copywriter").

% Constraint: interview on day 20 at "Laneplex" must occur.
interview(20, "Laneplex", P) :- positions(P).

% Constraint: If there is a "copywriter" interview on day D, then there must be a "web developer" interview on day D+2.
:- interview(D, C, "copywriter"), D1 = D + 2, not interview(D1, C1, "web developer").

% Constraint: If there is a "web developer" interview on day D1, then there must be a "copywriter" interview on day D1-2.
:- interview(D1, C1, "web developer"), D = D1 - 2, not interview(D, C, "copywriter").

% Ensure that each day has exactly one interview per company and position? No, multiple interviews possible per day, but we don't enforce uniqueness unless specified.

% The search space is generated by all possible combinations of (D, C, P) satisfying the above constraints.
% The above rules define the constraints that must be satisfied by any answer set.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
months(4; 5; 6; 7).
street("Aspen Court"; "Norway Court"; "Oak Road"; "Quince Street").
manager("Bill Bonner"; "Dawn Daniels"; "Freda Fisher"; "Irma Ingram").

% For each month, there is exactly one project with a unique street and manager.
% Each project assigns one street, one manager, and one month.
% Ensure that each month is associated with exactly one (street, manager) pair.
{project(Street, Manager, Month)}=1 :- months(Month), street(Street), manager(Manager).

% Enforce that each month is used exactly once.
:- months(Month), not {project(Street, Manager, Month)}=1.

% Enforce that each street is used exactly once.
:- street(Street), not {project(Street, Manager, Month)}=1.

% Enforce that each manager is used exactly once.
:- manager(Manager), not {project(Street, Manager, Month)}=1.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
scores(42; 49; 56; 63).
chef("Karl"; "Nelson"; "Peggy"; "Rosalie").
dish("cajun chili"; "cashew tofu"; "lemon snapper"; "smoked pork").

% Each chef C cooks exactly one dish D and has exactly one score S.
{cook(C, D, S): dish(D), scores(S)}=1 :- chef(C).

% Constraint: If two chefs C1 and C2 cook different dishes D1 and D2, then they must have different scores.
:- cook(C1, D1, S), cook(C2, D2, S), C1 != C2, D1 != D2.

% Optional: Ensure that each dish is cooked by exactly one chef.
{cook(C, D, S): chef(C), scores(S)}=1 :- dish(D).

% Optional: Ensure that each score is assigned to exactly one chef.
{cook(C, D, S): chef(C), dish(D)}=1 :- scores(S).

% One of two: For any two chefs C1, C2 and dishes D1, D2, if C1 cooks D1 and C2 cooks D2, then the pair (C1,C2) and (D1,D2) must satisfy some relation (e.g., not both same or both different, depending on context). 
% Since the exact meaning of one_of_two is unspecified, we assume it means that for any two distinct chefs and two distinct dishes, at least one of the assignments must not hold.
% This is a placeholder for a more specific constraint based on actual predicate semantics.
% Here we assume: If C1 != C2 and D1 != D2, then it cannot be that both cook(C1,D1,S1) and cook(C2,D2,S2) hold for any S1,S2 — but this is too strong.
% Instead, we interpret one_of_two(C1,C2,D1,D2) as a constraint that must be satisfied when C1 cooks D1 and C2 cooks D2.

% We define one_of_two as a fact or rule based on external knowledge. Since it's not specified, we treat it as a predicate that should be true in valid models.
% So we require that whenever cook(C1,D1,S1) and cook(C2,D2,S2) are both true and C1 != C2, D1 != D2, then one_of_two(C1,C2,D1,D2) must be true.

:- cook(C1, D1, S1), cook(C2, D2, S2), C1 != C2, D1 != D2, not one_of_two(C1, C2, D1, D2).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
prices(45; 60; 75; 90).
winners("Alejandro"; "Faye"; "Irma"; "Phillip").
butterflies("atlas"; "emperor"; "grayling"; "peacock").

% Define the core relation: auction(W, B, P) means winner W bought butterfly B for price P.
auction(W, B, P) :- winners(W), butterflies(B), prices(P).

% Each winner, butterfly, and price is used exactly once (bijection).
{auction(W, B, P) : butterflies(B), prices(P)} = 1 :- winners(W).
{auction(W, B, P) : winners(W), prices(P)} = 1 :- butterflies(B).
{auction(W, B, P) : winners(W), butterflies(B)} = 1 :- prices(P).

% Clue 1: Alejandro's purchase cost less than Irma's purchase.
:- auction("Alejandro", _, P1), auction("Irma", _, P2), P1 >= P2.

% Clue 2: The insect that sold for $90 was either the atlas or emperor butterfly.
:- prices(90), not (auction(_, "atlas", 90); auction(_, "emperor", 90)).

% Clue 3: The peacock butterfly cost more than Irma's purchase.
:- auction(_, "peacock", P1), auction("Irma", _, P2), P1 =< P2.

% Clue 4: Of Alejandro's and Irma's purchases, one was the emperor butterfly and the other sold for $45.
:- auction("Alejandro", B1, P1), auction("Irma", B2, P2),
   not ((B1 = "emperor" ∧ P2 = 45) ∨ (B2 = "emperor" ∧ P1 = 45)).

% Clue 5: The four butterflies were Phillip's purchase, the insect that sold for $60, the butterfly that sold for $45, and the butterfly that sold for $90.
% This implies that Phillip's purchase is one of the $45, $60, $90 items (not $75).
:- auction("Phillip", _, P), P = 75.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
prices(24; 25; 26; 27).
names("Frances"; "Hannah"; "Kurt"; "Olga").
wines("merlot"; "port"; "riesling"; "shiraz").

% Define the order predicate with constraints.
order(Name, Wine, Price) :- names(Name), wines(Wine), prices(Price).

% Constraint 1: Either (Frances orders something at 25, Olga orders shiraz) OR (Frances orders shiraz, Olga orders something at 25)
{order("Frances", _, 25); order("Olga", "shiraz", _)}=1.
{order("Frances", "shiraz", _); order("Olga", _, 25)}=1.

% Constraint 2: Olga's port order price equals Kurt's price + 2
:- order("Olga", "port", P), order("Kurt", _, K), P != K + 2.

% Constraint 3: Olga orders merlot
order("Olga", "merlot", _).

% Ensure all order instances are consistent with the defined domains
% (already enforced by the head of the rule and domain declarations)
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
name("Celia"; "Laura"; "Natalie"; "Roberta").
bird("lorikeet"; "lovebird"; "macaw"; "parrot").

% Define the ownership relation.
owns(Name, Bird, Month) :- name(Name), bird(Bird), months(Month).

% One of parrot or lorikeet was bought in April
{owns(_, "parrot", 4); owns(_, "lorikeet", 4)}.

% The other is Roberta's
{owns("Roberta", "parrot", _); owns("Roberta", "lorikeet", _)}.

% Exactly one of parrot or lorikeet is bought in April
:- owns(_, "parrot", 4), owns(_, "lorikeet", 4).
:- \+ owns(_, "parrot", 4), \+ owns(_, "lorikeet", 4).

% Exactly one of parrot or lorikeet is Roberta's
:- owns("Roberta", "parrot", _), owns("Roberta", "lorikeet", _).
:- \+ owns("Roberta", "parrot", _), \+ owns("Roberta", "lorikeet", _).

% Natalie bought something in month 2, Roberta in a later month
:- owns("Natalie", _, M1), owns("Roberta", _, M2), M1 >= M2.

% Laura owns the lovebird
owns("Laura", "lovebird", _).

% Either lorikeet or lovebird was bought in month 2
{owns(_, "lorikeet", 2); owns(_, "lovebird", 2)}.

% Either Natalie owns lorikeet and Roberta owns macaw in month 2, or Natalie owns macaw and Roberta owns something in month 2
{owns("Natalie", "lorikeet", _), owns("Roberta", "macaw", 2)} ; {owns("Natalie", "macaw", _), owns("Roberta", _, 2)}.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
clients("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% Define the assignment predicate: assign(masseuse, client, price)
assign(M, C, P) :- masseuse(M), clients(C), price(P).

% Each masseuse is assigned to exactly one client and one price (bijective mapping)
{assign(M, C, P) : clients(C), price(P)} = 1 :- masseuse(M).
{assign(M, C, P) : masseuse(M), price(P)} = 1 :- clients(C).
{assign(M, C, P) : masseuse(M), clients(C)} = 1 :- price(P).

% Clue 1: Hannah paid more than Teri's client
% Let P_H be price paid by Hannah, P_T be price paid by Teri's client
% Then P_H > P_T
:- assign(M_H, "Hannah", P_H), assign("Teri", C_T, P_T), P_H <= P_T.

% Clue 2: Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client
% P_F = P_L + 20
:- assign(M_F, "Freda", P_F), assign("Lynda", C_L, P_L), P_F != P_L + 20.

% Clue 3: Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client
% P_H = P_N - 10 → P_N = P_H + 10
:- assign(M_H, "Hannah", P_H), assign("Nancy", C_N, P_N), P_N != P_H + 10.

% Clue 4: Nancy's client, Hannah, and Ginger were all different clients
% So Nancy's client ≠ Hannah and ≠ Ginger
:- assign("Nancy", "Hannah", _).
:- assign("Nancy", "Ginger", _).

% Clue 5: Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client
% So either: assign(M, "Hannah", 180) OR assign("Lynda", "Hannah", P)
% We express the negation: if Hannah didn't pay 180 AND wasn't Lynda's client, then invalid
:- assign(M, "Hannah", P), P != 180, not assign("Lynda", "Hannah", _).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun").
year(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_color("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

% Main predicate: jumper(Name, Year, Color) — captures the full assignment.
jumper(Name, Year, Color) :- names(Name), year(Year), suit_color(Color).

% Ensure each name, year, and color is used exactly once.
:- #count{Y: jumper(Name, Y, _)} != 1, names(Name).
:- #count{C: jumper(Name, _, C)} != 1, names(Name).
:- #count{N: jumper(N, Y, _)} != 1, year(Y).
:- #count{N: jumper(N, _, C)} != 1, suit_color(C).

% Additional predicates for ordering relations between years.
% before(Y1, Y2) means Y1 < Y2
before(Y1, Y2) :- year(Y1), year(Y2), Y1 < Y2.

% oneYearBefore(Y1, Y2) means Y2 = Y1 + 1
oneYearBefore(Y1, Y2) :- year(Y1), year(Y2), Y2 = Y1 + 1.

% twoYearsBefore(Y1, Y2) means Y2 = Y1 + 2
twoYearsBefore(Y1, Y2) :- year(Y1), year(Y2), Y2 = Y1 + 2.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
teas("Ali Shan"; "Jin Xuan"; "Pouchong"; "Red Robe").
price(4; 5; 6; 7).
temperature(190; 195; 200; 215).

% For each tea T, it is brewed at exactly one price P and one temperature Temp.
{brew(T, P, Temp): price(P), temperature(Temp)}=1 :- teas(T).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Anna"; "Delores"; "Wilma"; "Yvonne").
scholarship(25000; 30000; 35000; 40000).
major("Biology"; "Mathematics"; "Physics"; "Theology").

% Each student is assigned exactly one name, one scholarship, and one major.
{student(Name, Scholarship, Major)} :- names(Name), scholarship(Scholarship), major(Major).

% Constraint: The student with major "Physics" has a scholarship 5000 more than the student with major "Biology".
student(_, S1, "Physics"), student(_, S2, "Biology"), S1 = S2 + 5000.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
winners("Brandon"; "Otis"; "Phillip"; "Rena").
price(45; 60; 75; 90).
butterfly("peacock"; "peppered"; "swallowtail"; "torturix").

% Define the single predicate that links winner, price, and butterfly.
auction(W, P, B) :- winners(W), price(P), butterfly(B).

% Each winner is assigned exactly one price and one butterfly.
{auction(W, P, B) : price(P), butterfly(B)} = 1 :- winners(W).

% Each price is assigned to exactly one winner and one butterfly.
{auction(W, P, B) : winners(W), butterfly(B)} = 1 :- price(P).

% Each butterfly is assigned to exactly one winner and one price.
{auction(W, P, B) : winners(W), price(P)} = 1 :- butterfly(B).

% Clue 1: The four butterflies were the swallowtail butterfly, Rena's purchase, the insect that sold for $90, and Phillip's purchase.
% This implies all four are distinct, so:
% - swallowtail ≠ Rena's purchase
% - swallowtail ≠ $90 item
% - swallowtail ≠ Phillip's purchase
% - Rena's purchase ≠ $90 item
% - Rena's purchase ≠ Phillip's purchase
% - $90 item ≠ Phillip's purchase
% We encode these as constraints:
:- auction(W, 90, "swallowtail").
:- auction("Rena", P, "swallowtail").
:- auction("Phillip", P, "swallowtail").
:- auction("Rena", 90, B).
:- auction("Phillip", P, B), auction("Rena", P, B).
:- auction("Phillip", P, B), auction("Rena", P, B), P = 90.

% Clue 2: Otis's purchase was either the peacock or the swallowtail butterfly.
:- auction("Otis", P, B), B != "peacock", B != "swallowtail".

% Clue 3: Brandon's purchase cost 30 dollars less than Otis's purchase.
:- auction("Brandon", P_B, _), auction("Otis", P_O, _), P_B != P_O - 30.

% Clue 4: Rena's purchase cost 15 dollars less than the swallowtail butterfly.
:- auction("Rena", P_R, _), auction(W, P_S, "swallowtail"), P_R != P_S - 15.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
breads("flatbread"; "pumpernickel"; "rye bread"; "wheat bread").
time("7:00am"; "7:30am"; "8:00am"; "8:30am").
customers("Felicia"; "Isabel"; "Ora"; "Tricia").

% Define the main predicate: order(C, B, T) means customer C ordered bread B at time T.
order(C, B, T) :- customers(C), breads(B), time(T).

% Each customer orders exactly one bread and one time.
{order(C, B, T) : breads(B), time(T)} = 1 :- customers(C).

% Each bread is ordered by exactly one customer.
{order(C, B, T) : customers(C), time(T)} = 1 :- breads(B).

% Each time has exactly one customer ordering.
{order(C, B, T) : customers(C), breads(B)} = 1 :- time(T).

% Clue 1: The person who came in at 8:00am asked for wheat bread.
:- not order(C, "wheat bread", "8:00am") :- customers(C).

% Clue 2: Tricia came in a half-hour after Ora.
:- not (order(Tricia, _, "7:30am") ; order(Tricia, _, "8:00am") ; order(Tricia, _, "8:30am")), order(Ora, _, "7:00am").
:- not (order(Tricia, _, "8:00am") ; order(Tricia, _, "8:30am")), order(Ora, _, "7:30am").
:- not (order(Tricia, _, "8:30am")), order(Ora, _, "8:00am").
:- order(Tricia, _, T1), order(Ora, _, T2), T1 = T2 + 30, T1 > "8:30am".
:- order(Tricia, _, T1), order(Ora, _, T2), T1 = T2 + 30, T2 = "8:30am".

% Clue 3: Of Felicia and the customer who ordered flatbread, one came in at 7:30am and the other at 7:00am.
% So they are different people, and their times are 7:00 and 7:30.
:- order(Felicia, _, T1), order(C, "flatbread", T2), T1 = T2.
:- order(Felicia, _, T1), order(C, "flatbread", T2), T1 = "7:00am", T2 = "7:00am".
:- order(Felicia, _, T1), order(C, "flatbread", T2), T1 = "7:30am", T2 = "7:30am".
:- order(Felicia, _, T1), order(C, "flatbread", T2), T1 = "8:00am", T2 = "8:00am".
:- order(Felicia, _, T1), order(C, "flatbread", T2), T1 = "8:30am", T2 = "8:30am".

% Clue 4: Of the customer who ordered wheat bread and the person who ordered pumpernickel, one came in at 8:30am and the other was Tricia.
% So either:
%   - wheat bread customer at 8:30am, pumpernickel customer is Tricia
%   - pumpernickel customer at 8:30am, wheat bread customer is Tricia
% But not both at 8:30am, and not both not Tricia.
:- order(C1, "wheat bread", "8:30am"), order(C2, "pumpernickel", T), C1 = C2.
:- order(C1, "wheat bread", T1), order(C2, "pumpernickel", T2), T1 = "8:30am", T2 = "8:30am".
:- order(C1, "wheat bread", T1), order(C2, "pumpernickel", T2), C1 = "Tricia", C2 = "Tricia".
:- order(C1, "wheat bread", T1), order(C2, "pumpernickel", T2), T1 = "8:30am", C2 = "Tricia".
:- order(C1, "wheat bread", T1), order(C2, "pumpernickel", T2), T2 = "8:30am", C1 = "Tricia".
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
dinosaurs("archadon"; "mirasaurus"; "pilodontus"; "senosaurus").
price(500; 750; 1000; 1250).
ages(69; 75; 78; 85).

% Define the fossil predicate: fossil(D, A, P) means dinosaur D has age A and price P.
% We generate all possible combinations of dinosaurs, ages, and prices.
fossil(D, A, P) :- dinosaurs(D), ages(A), price(P).

% The given constraint: there exists D1 such that fossil(D1, 75, P1) and fossil("pilodontus", A2, P2) with P1 = P2 - 500.
% This is encoded as a choice rule that ensures such a pair exists.
:- not exists_fossil_pair.

% Define the existence condition: there is some D1 with age 75 and some P1, and "pilodontus" with some A2 and P2 such that P1 = P2 - 500.
exists_fossil_pair :- fossil(D1, 75, P1), fossil("pilodontus", A2, P2), P1 = P2 - 500.
```
